epigraph.text: The heavens declare the glory of God, and the sky above proclaims his handiwork.
epigraph.quote: The greatest value of a picture is when it forces us to notice what we never expected to see.

content.text:
The human eye is remarkably adept at detecting patterns—far better than 
the mind is at scanning columns of numbers. A well-designed graph can 
reveal at a glance what might take pages of prose to describe.

For categorical variables, we count how many individuals fall into each 
category and display these counts (or proportions) visually.

content.title: Frequency and Relative Frequency
content.content:
The **frequency** of a category is the count of 
individuals in that category.

The **relative frequency** (or 
*proportion*) is the frequency divided by the total number of 
individuals:
\[
 \text{Relative frequency} = \frac{\text{Frequency}}{\text{Total count}}
\]

content.text:
**Bar Charts** display frequencies using rectangular bars, with 
height proportional to frequency. Bars are separated by gaps to 
emphasize that categories are distinct.

**Pie Charts** display relative frequencies as slices of a circle. 
While visually appealing, pie charts make it difficult to compare 
categories precisely and should generally be avoided for serious 
statistical work.

content.title: Creating a Bar Chart
content.problem:
A survey asked 200 students about their favorite season. The results 
were: Spring (52), Summer (78), Fall (48), Winter (22). Construct 
a bar chart.
content.solution:
center
tikzpicture
 axis[
 ybar,
 width=10cm,
 height=6cm,
 bar width=1cm,
 ymin=0,
 ymax=90,
 ylabel={Frequency},
 symbolic x coords={Spring, Summer, Fall, Winter},
 xtick=data,
 nodes near coords,
 nodes near coords align={vertical},
 enlarge x limits=0.2,
 ]
 coordinates {
 (Spring, 52) (Summer, 78) (Fall, 48) (Winter, 22)
 };
 axis
tikzpicture
center
The bar chart reveals that Summer is the most popular season, with 
nearly 40\% of responses, while Winter is least popular.

content.text:
For quantitative data, we want to see the **distribution**—the 
pattern of values the variable takes and how often each value (or 
range of values) occurs.

content.title: Distribution
content.content:
The **distribution** of a variable tells us 
what values the variable takes and how often it takes each value.

content.text:
A **dotplot** places a dot above the number line 
for each observation. When values repeat, dots are stacked vertically.

Dotplots work best for small datasets where individual values matter. 
They reveal clusters, gaps, and outliers clearly.

A **stemplot** splits each observation into a 
*stem* (leading digit(s)) and a *leaf* (trailing digit). 
The stems form a vertical column; leaves are written in order beside 
each stem.

content.title: Constructing a Stemplot
content.problem:
The following data give the ages of 20 attendees at a church 
committee meeting:
\[
 23, 45, 52, 38, 41, 55, 63, 47, 39, 42, 51, 44, 60, 35, 48, 57, 43, 49, 36, 54
\]
Construct a stemplot.
content.solution:
Using tens as stems and ones as leaves:

| Stem | Leaves |
| 2 | 3 |
| 3 | 5\,6\,8\,9 |
| 4 | 1\,2\,3\,4\,5\,7\,8\,9 |
| 5 | 1\,2\,4\,5\,7 |
| 6 | 0\,3 |

The stemplot shows that most attendees are in their 40s and 50s, 
with relatively few in their 20s or 60s.

content.text:
A **histogram** divides the range of data into 
intervals of equal width (called *bins* or *classes*) and 
displays the frequency or relative frequency of each interval as a 
bar. Unlike bar charts, histogram bars touch because the variable is 
continuous.

content.title: Choosing the Number of Bins
content.content:
Too few bins hide the shape of the distribution; too many create a 
noisy picture. A common rule of thumb is to use between 5 and 20 bins, 
with more bins for larger datasets. Experiment with different bin 
widths to find a display that reveals the distribution's structure.

content.text:
When examining the distribution of a quantitative variable, we focus 
on four characteristics:

items:
**Shape:** Is the distribution symmetric or skewed? 
 Unimodal, bimodal, or multimodal?
**Center:** Where is the "middle" of the data?
**Spread:** How variable are the data values?
**Unusual features:** Are there outliers, gaps, or clusters?

content.title: Shape of a Distribution
content.content:
A distribution is **symmetric** if 
the left and right sides are (approximately) mirror images.

A distribution is **skewed right** 
(positively skewed) if it has a long tail extending to the right.

A distribution is **skewed left** 
(negatively skewed) if it has a long tail extending to the left.

A **mode** is a peak in the distribution. Distributions 
may be unimodal (one peak), bimodal (two peaks), or multimodal.

content.title: Identifying Shape
content.problem:
The Old Faithful geyser in Yellowstone erupts at 
irregular intervals. A histogram of eruption durations shows two 
distinct peaks: one around 2 minutes and another around 4.5 minutes.

This distribution is **bimodal**. The two peaks suggest there 
may be two different "types" of eruptions governed by different 
underlying mechanisms—a pattern that reveals the complex geology of 
the geyser.

content.title: Outliers
content.content:
An **outlier** is an observation that falls 
substantially outside the overall pattern of the distribution.

content.text: Always investigate outliers. They may represent:

items: Genuine extreme values that reveal important phenomena
Data entry errors that should be corrected

content.text: Never simply delete outliers without justification.
